The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) signed into law by President Trump on July 4, 2025, introduces significant changes to the clean energy tax credit landscape established by the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA). The OBBBA enhances domestic content requirements and makes them mandatory for obtaining the IRA's Production Tax Credits (45Y) for projects beginning construction on or after June 16, 2025. Specifically, the law harmonizes the domestic content requirements under Section 48E with those under Section 45Y, ensuring that meeting domestic-content rules is a condition for claiming the 45Y Production Tax Credit. This information comes from credible sources such as Arnold & Porter and White & Case LLP, which provide detailed analyses of the legislative changes and their implications for clean energy projects.

The domestic content requirements for obtaining the IRA's Investment Tax Credits (48Ε) become mandatory in 2025, as evidenced by recent legislative changes. The Senate Finance Committee's reconciliation bill updates domestic content percentages for Section 48E projects and imposes stricter Foreign Entity of Concern (FEOC) restrictions. Projects failing to meet these thresholds will receive only a reduced credit, indicating that domestic-content compliance will become mandatory for full Investment Tax Credit eligibility in 2025.

The House of Representatives passed a reconciliation bill (H.R. 1) on May 22, 2025, which amends several Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) tax credits, including the Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit (Section 45X). The bill introduces new Foreign Entity of Concern (FEOC) restrictions that effectively require the use of domestic materials or subcomponents for certain tax credits. The FEOC rules disallow credits for taxpayers identified as specified foreign entities or foreign-influenced entities and for projects receiving material assistance from prohibited foreign entities after December 31, 2025. These rules significantly impact the Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit by restricting the eligibility of components produced with foreign assistance.
